statement: The 29 August 2026 Icelandic referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations is advisory (ráðgefandi), not binding. Iceland's constitution does not provide for binding referenda — all referenda under Icelandic law are advisory to the Althingi, which retains the final legislative authority. Article 26 of the constitution allows the President to refer legislation to a national vote, but this has only been used once (in 2010, for the Icesave dispute). The 2026 referendum is called by ordinary legislation, not under Article 26. Despite being legally advisory, the political reality is that the government has committed to respect the result. The 2012 constitutional referendum showed the ambiguity: 67% voted to use the Constitutional Council's draft as the basis for a new constitution, but the Althingi never acted on the result, citing 49% turnout and procedural concerns. A clear majority on 29 August would create strong political pressure but no legal obligation to act.
source_name: Stjórnarskrá lýðveldisins Íslands (Constitution of Iceland), Articles 26, 65, 79; Althingi records on 2012 referendum follow-up
caveats: The 2012 precedent cuts both ways: it shows advisory referenda can be ignored, but it also generated lasting public anger about democratic legitimacy. If turnout is high and the margin clear, ignoring the result would be politically very difficult. The coalition parties have explicitly committed to resuming negotiations if the 'yes' side wins. The threshold for what constitutes a 'clear' result has not been defined — a narrow victory could create procedural and political uncertainty.

related_entries.statement: On 20 October 2012, Iceland held a six-question advisory referendum on constitutional reform proposals drafted by the Stjórnlagaráð (Constitutional Council). The first and most significant question — 'Do you wish the proposals of the Constitutional Council to form the basis of a new Constitution?' — received 66.9% yes votes (49,486 yes vs 24,521 no). All six questions received majority yes votes. Turnout was 48.9% (115,890 of approximately 237,000 eligible voters), significantly lower than typical parliamentary election turnout of 80–85%. The referendum was advisory (ráðgefandi), not legally binding, as the Icelandic constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions. Despite the clear majority in favour, no subsequent Alþingi has enacted the draft into law.

The Icelandic government announced in early 2026 that a national referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations would be held on 29 August 2026. The referendum is based on a parliamentary resolution (þingsályktunartillaga) submitted by the Foreign Minister. The three coalition parties — Samfylkingin (Social Democrats), Viðreisn (Reform Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) — support holding the referendum. The referendum question concerns whether Iceland should resume the accession negotiations that were suspended in 2013, not whether Iceland should join the EU outright. This is an advisory referendum (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), as Icelandic constitutional law does not provide for binding referenda on policy questions (the only binding referendum mechanism is under Article 26 for presidential vetoes of legislation).

Following the 2012 advisory referendum in which 66.9% supported using the Constitutional Council's draft as the basis for a new constitution, successive Alþingi sessions have failed to enact constitutional reform. The centre-right government of Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson and Bjarni Benediktsson (2013–2016) effectively shelved the draft, arguing it needed further expert review. Subsequent governments (2016–2017, 2017–2021, 2021–2024) introduced partial constitutional bills — notably on natural resources and electoral reform — but none passed into law. The Stjórnarskrárfélagið (Constitutional Society) and other civil society organisations have campaigned to honour the referendum result, framing parliamentary inaction as a democratic deficit. As of early 2026, no provisions of the 2011 draft have been enacted, making it one of the most protracted constitutional reform efforts in modern European democracies.

citations.report_title: Sameign þjóðarinnar — eða stærsta tilfærsla auðlinda í sögu Íslands?

claims.claim_text: Þegar tekist var á um Icesave-málið var það ekki Alþingi sem lagði málið í dóm þjóðarinnar — það gerðist aðeins eftir að forseti Íslands neitaði að staðfesta lögin.
